APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NMT modeling problem (hierarchical decoding) using standard datasets (ASPEC) and WAT’16 evaluation with appropriate metrics (BLEU, RIBES) and competitive baselines, so the topic and audience clearly lie within top-tier NLP/ML venues. From a reproducibility-engineer perspective it generally meets venue expectations for problem formulation and evaluation standards, but there are minor scope concerns: experiments are limited to English–Japanese (first 2M pairs only), code availability isn’t mentioned, and variance/statistical testing across runs is not reported, which would strengthen its suitability for a top conference. | B(5): This paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core MT problem (hierarchical decoding to model chunk-level vs word-level generation) that is of clear interest to the machine-translation community. The methodology (three progressively enriched chunk-based decoders) and evaluations on ASPEC with WAT’16 test sets using BLEU and RIBES and comparisons to strong seq2seq and tree-to-seq baselines match community standards. The formulation, datasets, and metrics target the right audience and the work is positioned within relevant prior NMT and structured-decoder research, making it appropriate for a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NMT modeling problem (hierarchical decoding) using standard datasets (ASPEC) and WAT’16 evaluation with appropriate metrics (BLEU, RIBES) and competitive baselines, so the topic and audience clearly lie within top-tier NLP/ML venues. From a reproducibility-engineer perspective it generally meets venue expectations for problem formulation and evaluation standards, but there are minor scope concerns: experiments are limited to English–Japanese (first 2M pairs only), code availability isn’t mentioned, and variance/statistical testing across runs is not reported, which would strengthen its suitability for a top conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core MT problem (hierarchical decoding to model chunk-level vs word-level generation) that is of clear interest to the machine-translation community. The methodology (three progressively enriched chunk-based decoders) and evaluations on ASPEC with WAT’16 test sets using BLEU and RIBES and comparisons to strong seq2seq and tree-to-seq baselines match community standards. The formulation, datasets, and metrics target the right audience and the work is positioned within relevant prior NMT and structured-decoder research, making it appropriate for a top conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clearly articulated chunk-based hierarchical decoder that separates chunk-level (global) and word-level (local) generation and further refines it with inter-chunk connections and word-to-chunk feedback (Models 1–3). This is a notable, non-trivial extension beyond standard seq-to-seq and tree-to-seq baselines reported, and the authors explicitly differentiate the three variants and the problems they address (inter-chunk context and feedback). From a reproducibility/engineering perspective the idea is novel enough to inform experts, though it reads as an evolution of hierarchical decoding rather than a radical paradigm shift. | B(3): The paper proposes a hierarchical chunk-based decoder that separates chunk-level (global) and word-level (local) generation and introduces two additional mechanisms (inter-chunk connections and word-to-chunk feedback). While the explicit chunking and the specific three-model progression address documented limitations (e.g., missing previous-chunk info and lack of feedback) and are clearly differentiated from the compared tree/word baselines, hierarchical/structured decoders and multi-scale generation are already known in MT literature. The extensions are non-trivial engineering/modeling steps but read as an incremental, targeted improvement rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so the contribution would be moderately informative to an expert rather than surprising.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a clearly articulated chunk-based hierarchical decoder that separates chunk-level (global) and word-level (local) generation and further refines it with inter-chunk connections and word-to-chunk feedback (Models 1–3). This is a notable, non-trivial extension beyond standard seq-to-seq and tree-to-seq baselines reported, and the authors explicitly differentiate the three variants and the problems they address (inter-chunk context and feedback). From a reproducibility/engineering perspective the idea is novel enough to inform experts, though it reads as an evolution of hierarchical decoding rather than a radical paradigm shift.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a hierarchical chunk-based decoder that separates chunk-level (global) and word-level (local) generation and introduces two additional mechanisms (inter-chunk connections and word-to-chunk feedback). While the explicit chunking and the specific three-model progression address documented limitations (e.g., missing previous-chunk info and lack of feedback) and are clearly differentiated from the compared tree/word baselines, hierarchical/structured decoders and multi-scale generation are already known in MT literature. The extensions are non-trivial engineering/modeling steps but read as an incremental, targeted improvement rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so the contribution would be moderately informative to an expert rather than surprising.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles a meaningful engineering problem—incorporating chunk-level structure into NMT decoders—and demonstrates clear BLEU/RIBES improvements on the ASPEC/WAT’16 English→Japanese tasks using three progressively improved models (Models 1–3) compared against strong word- and tree-based baselines. From a reproducibility/impact standpoint the idea is practically relevant and likely to inspire follow-up work on hierarchical decoders and inter-chunk/feedback mechanisms, but the evaluation is limited to one language pair and datasets, code release is not mentioned, and broader adoption will depend on additional validations (more languages, variance estimates, runtime/complexity) before it changes prevailing practice. Overall this is a useful, moderate-impact contribution that opens directions but is not yet field-changing. | B(3): The paper tackles a meaningful practical problem—modeling multi-word chunks via a hierarchical chunk+word decoder—which is relevant for addressing coherence and local/global generation in NMT, and it shows empirical gains on ASPEC and the WAT ’16 English–Japanese task (BLEU/RIBES) with three progressively stronger models. These results are likely to influence researchers exploring structured decoders and hierarchical generation, but impact is moderate because evaluation is limited to one language pair/corpus, code availability is unclear, and broader generalization (other languages, domains, and tasks) is not demonstrated. Consequently the work is useful and likely to be built upon but not yet transformative across the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful engineering problem—incorporating chunk-level structure into NMT decoders—and demonstrates clear BLEU/RIBES improvements on the ASPEC/WAT’16 English→Japanese tasks using three progressively improved models (Models 1–3) compared against strong word- and tree-based baselines. From a reproducibility/impact standpoint the idea is practically relevant and likely to inspire follow-up work on hierarchical decoders and inter-chunk/feedback mechanisms, but the evaluation is limited to one language pair and datasets, code release is not mentioned, and broader adoption will depend on additional validations (more languages, variance estimates, runtime/complexity) before it changes prevailing practice. Overall this is a useful, moderate-impact contribution that opens directions but is not yet field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful practical problem—modeling multi-word chunks via a hierarchical chunk+word decoder—which is relevant for addressing coherence and local/global generation in NMT, and it shows empirical gains on ASPEC and the WAT ’16 English–Japanese task (BLEU/RIBES) with three progressively stronger models. These results are likely to influence researchers exploring structured decoders and hierarchical generation, but impact is moderate because evaluation is limited to one language pair/corpus, code availability is unclear, and broader generalization (other languages, domains, and tasks) is not demonstrated. Consequently the work is useful and likely to be built upon but not yet transformative across the field.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper proposes three controlled model variants (Models 1–3) and reports BLEU/RIBES improvements on ASPEC and WAT’16 English–Japanese, which gives a reasonable initial evaluation; however experiments are limited to one primary dataset subset, comparisons rely on prior reported baselines rather than strictly identical reimplemented baselines, and no run-to-run variance or statistical significance is reported. While some ablation is implicit in the three-model progression, there are no sensitivity analyses (e.g., chunk size, capacity, or training stability) or detailed failure-case analysis, and code availability is not mentioned, reducing reproducibility. Overall the empirical work is adequate to support the main claim directionally but lacks the depth and reproducibility rigor I expect for strong evidence. | B(4): The paper provides solid experimental evidence on a major EN–JP corpus (ASPEC) and evaluation on WAT’16, using BLEU and RIBES and comparisons to multiple strong baselines; the three proposed model variants also serve as controlled architectural ablations addressing inter-chunk and feedback effects. Related work coverage is described as adequate and known limitations (e.g., Model 1’s lack of chunk-history and the need for feedback) are discussed. However, there is no mention of broader sensitivity analyses (e.g., chunk-size effects, ablation of specific components beyond the three variants), nor is code availability reported which limits reproducibility despite hyperparameters being provided — hence a solid but not exhaustive empirical evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes three controlled model variants (Models 1–3) and reports BLEU/RIBES improvements on ASPEC and WAT’16 English–Japanese, which gives a reasonable initial evaluation; however experiments are limited to one primary dataset subset, comparisons rely on prior reported baselines rather than strictly identical reimplemented baselines, and no run-to-run variance or statistical significance is reported. While some ablation is implicit in the three-model progression, there are no sensitivity analyses (e.g., chunk size, capacity, or training stability) or detailed failure-case analysis, and code availability is not mentioned, reducing reproducibility. Overall the empirical work is adequate to support the main claim directionally but lacks the depth and reproducibility rigor I expect for strong evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid experimental evidence on a major EN–JP corpus (ASPEC) and evaluation on WAT’16, using BLEU and RIBES and comparisons to multiple strong baselines; the three proposed model variants also serve as controlled architectural ablations addressing inter-chunk and feedback effects. Related work coverage is described as adequate and known limitations (e.g., Model 1’s lack of chunk-history and the need for feedback) are discussed. However, there is no mention of broader sensitivity analyses (e.g., chunk-size effects, ablation of specific components beyond the three variants), nor is code availability reported which limits reproducibility despite hyperparameters being provided — hence a solid but not exhaustive empirical evaluation.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (chunk-based decoder and three model variants), with clear motivation and stated limitations. Writing quality is reported as good and related work/hyperparameters are adequately covered, which aids reproducibility. However, the summary does not indicate whether notation and figures/tables fully clarify the hierarchical decoder interactions (inter-chunk and feedback mechanisms), and the lack of mentioned code release or detailed visual examples may leave minor clarity gaps. From a reproducibility-engineer perspective this is a solid presentation with only modest presentation shortcomings. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation to hierarchical method descriptions (Models 1–3) and experiments on ASPEC/WAT16, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and methodological components (chunk-/word-level GRUs, inter-chunk and word-to-chunk feedback) are presented clearly and hyperparameters are reported, making reproduction plausible. Figures/tables and examples appear to support the claims and the writing is overall good, though a few minor presentation details (e.g., explicit pointer to code or slightly tighter discussion of limitations) could improve clarity further.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (chunk-based decoder and three model variants), with clear motivation and stated limitations. Writing quality is reported as good and related work/hyperparameters are adequately covered, which aids reproducibility. However, the summary does not indicate whether notation and figures/tables fully clarify the hierarchical decoder interactions (inter-chunk and feedback mechanisms), and the lack of mentioned code release or detailed visual examples may leave minor clarity gaps. From a reproducibility-engineer perspective this is a solid presentation with only modest presentation shortcomings.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation to hierarchical method descriptions (Models 1–3) and experiments on ASPEC/WAT16, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and methodological components (chunk-/word-level GRUs, inter-chunk and word-to-chunk feedback) are presented clearly and hyperparameters are reported, making reproduction plausible. Figures/tables and examples appear to support the claims and the writing is overall good, though a few minor presentation details (e.g., explicit pointer to code or slightly tighter discussion of limitations) could improve clarity further.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear claims and provides experimental evidence (BLEU/RIBES on ASPEC and WAT’16 En→Ja) and ablation-like comparisons across three model variants, which supports the main ideas. However, based on the summary there is insufficient information about controlled baselines (identical training/configuration), variance across runs or statistical significance, and no mention of released code, which weakens reproducibility and confidence in the robustness of the gains. Mathematical components appear straightforward and complete for the proposed hierarchical decoder, but the experimental methodology lacks some crucial controls and significance testing, so the conclusions are plausible but not fully convincing. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clear and supported by experiments showing improvement on WAT ’16 English–Japanese and ASPEC data, and the hierarchical chunk/word GRU formulations and three model variants directly address the stated inter-chunk and feedback issues. There are no reported mathematical derivation problems in the summary, and reasonable baselines (word/char and tree-to-seq models) and metrics (BLEU, RIBES) were used. However, the evaluation appears limited to a single language pair and a subset of ASPEC, and the summary does not report statistical significance or broader ablation analyses, leaving some evidence gaps about generality and robustness. Overall the technical claims are credible but would benefit from additional experimental validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides experimental evidence (BLEU/RIBES on ASPEC and WAT’16 En→Ja) and ablation-like comparisons across three model variants, which supports the main ideas. However, based on the summary there is insufficient information about controlled baselines (identical training/configuration), variance across runs or statistical significance, and no mention of released code, which weakens reproducibility and confidence in the robustness of the gains. Mathematical components appear straightforward and complete for the proposed hierarchical decoder, but the experimental methodology lacks some crucial controls and significance testing, so the conclusions are plausible but not fully convincing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clear and supported by experiments showing improvement on WAT ’16 English–Japanese and ASPEC data, and the hierarchical chunk/word GRU formulations and three model variants directly address the stated inter-chunk and feedback issues. There are no reported mathematical derivation problems in the summary, and reasonable baselines (word/char and tree-to-seq models) and metrics (BLEU, RIBES) were used. However, the evaluation appears limited to a single language pair and a subset of ASPEC, and the summary does not report statistical significance or broader ablation analyses, leaving some evidence gaps about generality and robustness. Overall the technical claims are credible but would benefit from additional experimental validation.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against sensible, recent baselines (word-based and tree-to-seq models from 2016) on the same ASPEC/WAT’16 English–Japanese data using BLEU and RIBES, and reports hyperparameters, so comparisons appear broadly fair. It also presents controlled variants (Models 1–3) that help isolate the effects of inter-chunk connections and word-to-chunk feedback. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance, multiple-run variance, or other strong baselines/ensembles common in translation evaluations, so there remain minor gaps in rigor and completeness of comparison. | B(4): The paper compares against reasonable and contemporary baselines (word-based Seq2Seq and Tree-to-Seq models from 2016) on the same ASPEC/WAT’16 English–Japanese data and uses the same BLEU/RIBES metrics, indicating largely fair experimental conditions. The manuscript clearly describes differences among its three chunk-based variants and discusses specific limitations (e.g., lack of inter-chunk context and need for feedback), and related work coverage is reported as adequate. It does not appear to omit major competing paradigms entirely, but given the claim of outperforming the best WAT’16 single NMT model, a more explicit acknowledgement or deeper analysis when prior methods perform comparably would strengthen the comparison completeness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against sensible, recent baselines (word-based and tree-to-seq models from 2016) on the same ASPEC/WAT’16 English–Japanese data using BLEU and RIBES, and reports hyperparameters, so comparisons appear broadly fair. It also presents controlled variants (Models 1–3) that help isolate the effects of inter-chunk connections and word-to-chunk feedback. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance, multiple-run variance, or other strong baselines/ensembles common in translation evaluations, so there remain minor gaps in rigor and completeness of comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against reasonable and contemporary baselines (word-based Seq2Seq and Tree-to-Seq models from 2016) on the same ASPEC/WAT’16 English–Japanese data and uses the same BLEU/RIBES metrics, indicating largely fair experimental conditions. The manuscript clearly describes differences among its three chunk-based variants and discusses specific limitations (e.g., lack of inter-chunk context and need for feedback), and related work coverage is reported as adequate. It does not appear to omit major competing paradigms entirely, but given the claim of outperforming the best WAT’16 single NMT model, a more explicit acknowledgement or deeper analysis when prior methods perform comparably would strengthen the comparison completeness.

main_claims: Propose chunk-based decoders for NMT that separate chunk-level (global) and word-level (local) generation.
Introduce three models: Model 1 (standard chunk-based NMT), Model 2 (adds inter-chunk connections), and Model 3 (adds word-to-chunk feedback) to address inter-chunk and feedback issues.

Demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves translation performance on a WAT ’16 English-to-Japanese task and that the best model outperforms the best single NMT model reported in WAT ’16.
_summary.methodology: Introduce a hierarchical decoder composed of a chunk-level GRU decoder that produces chunk representations and a word-level GRU decoder that generates words within each chunk; Model 2 adds inter-chunk GRU connections and Model 3 adds word-to-chunk feedback by updating chunk states with word-level states.
datasets: Asian Scientific Paper Excerpt Corpus (ASPEC) English-Japanese (used first 2 million of 3 million pairs)
WAT ’16 English–Japanese evaluation set (used for evaluation procedure)
limitations: In Model 1, the word-level decoder cannot access previous chunk information at the first word of each chunk.
Without feedback, the chunk-level decoder may fail to remove information about previous outputs, potentially generating the same chunks repeatedly or omitting source words.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducibility Engineer
reviewer_a.background: ML engineer and researcher with 8+ years building and evaluating neural machine translation systems, focusing on experimental rigor, benchmarking, and production-ready implementations.
traits: insists on strong baselines (identical training/configuration) and clear implementation details to reproduce results
requires ablation studies and controlled experiments to attribute improvements to specific model components
looks for statistical significance testing, variance estimates across runs, and clear hyperparameter reporting
values open-source releases, runtime/complexity analysis, and sane engineering assessments (inference speed, memory)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical and detail-oriented: I focus on whether the experiments support the claims and whether others can reproduce and adopt the method.

reviewer_b.name: Machine Translation Research Lead
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with a decade of publications in machine translation and sequence modeling, familiar with hierarchical/structured decoders and the WAT evaluation landscape.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior hierarchical/chunking and structured-decoder work and checks whether conceptual contributions are substantial
evaluates broader impact: whether the idea addresses an unmet need in MT (e.g., long-range coherence, morphology-rich languages) and its generalizability across language pairs
examines evaluation choices (datasets, metrics) and requests linguistically informed analysis (error types, examples) that demonstrate real improvements
considers clarity of motivation and whether the proposed models open promising future research directions
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Context-driven and strategic: I emphasize novelty, relevance to the MT community, and whether the results and analyses convincingly show a useful advance.
